speaker: interviewee
text: So, when I met Wacek, he told me that he's going, Saturday, home and I should go with him. It will be easier for me to travel. So I decided to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Saturday morning, he-- he picked me up. We walked to the station. And we-- we came into Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had about four hours of layover. So he said that he had a sister and he's going to-- sister. And then we'll meet-- he'll meet me half an hour before the train is going to leave. We left our luggage there, in-- at the station to guard it. And he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: As I was sitting on the station in Warsaw by myself, approximately, maybe 15, 20 minutes, all of a sudden, a thought came to my mind. Dummy, why are you sitting here? I'm in Warsaw. Why can't I go see, to the neighborhood where I lived, to see if somebody is there, what happened, if the house still standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And automatically, I left the station. As I was coming close to the neighborhood where I lived, approaching me came a man which I recognized right away, and grabbed him by his hand-- by his arm, actually. And I became so emotionally that I couldn't get out the word from me. So he come--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a father of a girlfriend I went to school with, from the neighborhood. So he told me to calm down and asked me how he can help me. So I called him by his name. His name was Jaszczemski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say, finally, Jaszczemski, do you recognize me? He said, no. I told him who I was. I ask him about my family. And he assured me that nobody is left. So I ask about my neighbor. What did you feel at that point-- moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] What I felt-- [SIGHS] sadness. And to tell the truth, I didn't feel much. Because I was very emotionally. Because I was in the neighborhood where I grew up. And I was right there. And all of a sudden, I ask him about my neighbors, because they were my last link to my family before the war start with the Germans. And he told me where she is. I don't even remember if I told him thank you. And I start running. As I came to the house where he told me my neighbor lives--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were crying the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, no. I wasn't crying. I was running. I came there. I looked at the list from the tenants. And I saw where she lives. I went to the stairs. And it was dark because it was-- everything was boarded up with boards, cardboard, because after the war was dark. And I thought to myself, I'm going to climb the steps. I kept one hand on the rail, the other on the wall. If I'll touch a-- a door, I'll knock on it. As I was climbing up the steps, a door opened. And a little light that fell from the room lit my neighbor's name. And after almost seven years, I recognized her right away. And I screamed, good morning, Mrs. Wotkowska. She recognized me by my voice. She screamed, Jesus, Mary, Sala. As she said those words, I fell. I fainted. She kneeled over me. She says, you're so lucky. Your sister Regina is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came to, I told her she's lying. She wants to make me feel good. And she says, no. Then I ask, where is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, I don't know exactly. She came to see me Easter time. Being she was the one that she was saving my sister, she was taking her from place to place. But that's a different story. Finally, she told me another neighbor, name [NON-ENGLISH], she knows. Because she was the last one that took her to the place where she used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought, what can I do now? I wanted to send word to my husband that I'm going to search for my sister. There was no communication. So I took my neighbor. I went out on the street. I stopped a horse and buggy. We went back to the station. And I was unpatiently waited for Wacek.

speaker: interviewee
text: But looks like Wacek got sick-- got drunk at his sister. Maybe got sick or fell asleep. The train left and Wacek didn't come. They did not want to give me my luggage-- the guy that was at the storage room. I didn't know how can I send word to my husband. Finally, took a little persuasion, he opened the door. And I said, I want only one suitcase. I'm going to leave the other one. He had the ticket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I said, how-- what can I do? At this moment, I really couldn't think. I saw on the floor a brown bag, like you put food in it. I grabbed the bag. I scribbled a note to my husband, that I found Regina and I'm going to search for her. I tie it to his suitcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought whenever he'll come, as soon as he pick up the suitcase, he'll see the note. And that's exactly what happened. He came home and he showed my husband the note I scribbled. I went back with my neighbors. We picked up the other neighbor. We went to the streetcar, took a streetcar and were going to Praga, which is on the other side of Vistula.

speaker: interviewer
text: I ask her, [NON-ENGLISH], where did you leave her? I don't know the street. I don't know the address. But as soon as I see a red wall, I know that's-- the next street, I left her.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took probably about 3/4 of an hour. Finally, I look through the window and I see a red wall. And she says, that's where we have to get off. I couldn't think. I had eyes red. My hair were not combed. I looked like a mental patient. When we start walking, my feet became paralyzed. She said she left them in a little house that was on the next street. She knocked at the door. Somebody came out. She-- her name was Jasia Dobrowolska. She ask if she lives there. And the lady said, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she live? She says, I don't know. We were standing and I was crying. We were-- my neighbor, her husband, and another neighbor, and myself-- we were crying. Everything-- in just a split of a second, a little girl passed by. She stopped by and she says, what are you doing here, lady? Who are you looking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't talk. But my neighbor said, we're looking for Jasia Dobrowolska that used to live here. She says, I know where she lives. She lives with the principal from the school. And she took us there. When she brought us there, when they knocked at the door-- I was immobile-- they knocked at the door. The lady opened the door. She looked at me. She says, you don't have to tell me. I look at you. I could see you her sister. If you wouldn't be her sister, I wouldn't tell you where she is. But being that you her sister, I have no right to forbid her to see. Please come in. As we walked into the house, the first thing I saw was her picture from communion, in a white dress and veil, standing on the dresser. I broke down. And then she told me that--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you broke down. Could you describe why you broke down?

speaker: interviewee
text: I broke down because my emotion was-- I broke down for happiness. That my sister is alive. It was Saturday. By then, it was Saturday evening. She told us that she's in a Girl Scout camp, which was-- by the river in Plonsk. I can't remember how-- how far it was from Warsaw. And she says, there's one thing I could tell you. I would have gone with you tomorrow, Sunday. But my son is in a different camp. So my-- my neighbor is going tomorrow to visit her daughter. She could go with you. I agreed and didn't think of anything, that I could be killed. I remained there. It was after the [NON-ENGLISH] program. I gave money my neighbors to take a buggy home-- home. And I remained overnight there. We both didn't sleep. I was crying for happiness. She was crying because she knew she was gonna lose her. And then, around 4 o'clock in the morning, the lady knocked at the door and we left. Before we left, she told me, I have one wish to tell you. Please don't tell her what to do. Let her do what she feels to do. Don't-- other words, don't give her any ideas. I promised her. We went to the bus. I can't remember how long it took-- maybe an hour, maybe two. We arrived to-- to Plonsk. Was around 6 o'clock in the morning, approximately, maybe 6:30. We ask where the camp is. And we were shown the road to camp. As we came close to the camp, maybe about 30 feet before-- it was Sunday morning-- all the girls were going-- running out on the tent and standing-- forming four in a row to go to church, with a flag. As soon as my sister ran out from the camp, after almost seven years, when I saw her, I was paralyzed and I couldn't walk. She was running in and out, in and out. And I watched her. And the lady suggested for me to stay-- there was a big tree by the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Sunday morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember the date. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- that was in 1946. Was-- I believe it was in August. July or August. And I-- she says to me, stay here, by the-- you know, behind the tree. And I'm going and tell the counselors to give her something to do, to leave her there, not to take her to church. So she did. And she-- and I watched her. And as she, the lady, the neighbor, was coming close, she saw her. I saw her, jumped on her, and hug her, and so did her daughter. Because it was the neighbor where she lived. And I watched her going in and out. And finally-- to this day, I have no recollection when I moved out from beneath the tree. I also brought a bag of candies and fruit and everything. I had it in my hand. And I walked. I couldn't-- did not go to her. I did not realize what I was doing. I simply passed her by. But as I was passing her by, she looked at me and looked at me. She didn't realize where did I come from. I passed her by. I was maybe 10 feet pass her by, all of a sudden, she jump me from behind. And she grabbed me. And she screamed, Sala. And I fainted. When I fainted, was a commotion, because there was about 150 girls. I remember somebody dragged me into the tent, put me down on the floor, and every time I opened my-- they-- the nurse gave me something. And every time I opened my eyes, she was kneeling and making the sign of cross. She was crossing herself, and then putting her head down, because she looked at me. She remind herself that she is Jewish. And after a few minutes, I came to. We went outside, sat by the river, talked a little bit. And then I said to her-- I couldn't tell her what I really wanted to, because I promised the lady that I'm not going to dictate to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to her, I live here, like, in [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm going to leave you money. When you come back from the camp and you want to come to see me, my husband, [INAUDIBLE], is there. And I have two children. And I want you to come visit them. So any time will be convenient for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, sure. She said, but you stay here a few minutes. I just have to go do something in the tent. Took about 15 minutes, not even that. She came out with her backpack all packed. I'm going with you. Of course, I was so happy. I was so glad. And nobody could say that I dictated her. We took the bus back, came into the house where she was. And the lady says, you don't have to tell me. I could see. She was crying. We were all crying. I told her, don't take anything. You'll have everything. Just come. I took my sister, went to my neighbors. When they saw us, they-- they were so happy. They didn't let us go home. We had-- we had to take the train because her daughter lived in Lodz. I can't remember how far it is from Warsaw. So we had to take the train and go to Lodz. So I was-- by the time I came home, I was missing 11 days. Bringing my sister home on the train, there was thousands of people. There was a layover. And I told everybody that I'm bringing my sister home. At that time, to bring a Jewish child from a Christian home without anything, it was next to impossible. People that had survived in, let's say, a house-- a building would survive, they would want to give away the building to have a child and they never got it. Here, I brought my sister without anything. She was 15 and 1/2 years old. When we got to the station, my husband was at the station. Luckily, we didn't miss each other. Because our train came half an hour before he said to leave. He left the children with friends. He was going to look for me. Because he was sure that I was killed. There was no communication. When we got off the train, and he saw her-- because I say, don't go with me. I want to see if he'll recognize you. So I left first. She left second. He recognized you. The whole city knew that I found her sister. It was a small community. So everybody, what we came back, knew each other. On the way home, I had a procession. You know. All the people, everybody was crying. To make the story short, she came to my house. And my son got sick. My son were-- was taken to the hospital, which-- he came down with typhus. He was in a hospital. The hospital was about two streets away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the same time that I brought my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What son?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- Jacob. He was in the hospital. My sister was in my house, maybe, two weeks. One morning, we get up.

speaker: interviewer
text: My sister sits and crying. She wants to go home. Why are you crying? I want to go home. Why you want to go home? I'm here. My husband is here. My children are here. This is your home. No, I want to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband said, don't let her out of your sight. We locked the door for the night. We took the key out. If she went to the bathroom-- the bathroom is-- was outside-- one of us would stay in front of the bathroom-- or my friend. Wherever she went, she was never left alone. Until my husband's brother came over, to be together. And I can't remember how it was arranged. To the-- it was organization called [NON-ENGLISH]. They arranged to leave. And about 15 minutes before we had to be in a certain place, I took a blanket. I went up to the hospital. I kidnapped my son and we escaped. We escape Poland to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia to Austria. We were in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sister, with my brother-- with my brother-in-law and his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to take her with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. She was with me. She-- she-- she had no choice. Because we didn't let her go. Once we were in Vienna, I wasn't afraid for her. She was with me. She was glad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what-- when-- when did you find out what happened to the rest of the family, to your parents and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I found out from my sister that my brother was shot. He came to my neighbors for food. They gave him food-- with the children. On the way back to the ghetto, he was shot by the wall from the ghetto. The children escaped. My mother died from hunger. And that's when she told the children, try to escape to go to my neighbors. That's how my sister was saved through my neighbors. My sister, Hana, was in the church. The priest that used to teach religion the school I was attending to, he kept her for quite some time. But my sister, when it became very dangerous for my neighbors-- because some were good. Some were afraid that the-- they were-- they'll be shot by the neighbors because of Jewish child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of my neighbors took her to a friend of hers, to Praga. That's where I found her. But she took her to a friend that she knew, that she didn't know nothing about her. She was working for Gestapo. She was taking in Jews, keeping him for a week or two, taking everything away, and then the Gestapo would come in and take him away and they disappeared. My sister would-- my older sister, Hana-- she would risk her life during the curfew hours, to come to see my younger sister. One day, the lady said, why should your sister risk her life? She could stay with you. And she was with her. She left the church, came to my sister, was with her, until one day, the Gestapo came in and take-- took her out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister-- my-- Regina told me that when the Gestapo came in, she didn't hug her or kiss her. She was-- you know, she was-- my older one was afraid-- my-- Hana was afraid for Regina not to show the Gestapo that maybe she's her sister. All she did, she gave her her-- shook her hand, and said, maybe we'll see each other someday. And that's when she saw her the last time. See, I-- when we were in Austria, I wrote to the these-- to the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], to [NON-ENGLISH], to compensate the lady, because we took her without anything. No, no, nothing. She didn't do anything to stop us. So then, we got a letter from her that they gave her a lot of money from the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], which we were very happy. She wrote she bought a radio and some other things to remember her by.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when your sister Regina told you about what happened to your family, how did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Affect me very much. We'd often talk about it. My sister could never talk about it, what happened to her, what she lived through, until recently, until she made a video for Shoah. Even I had to tell her story to her children, because she couldn't talk about it. I'm more hardened. I could talk about it. See, from-- from Vienna, we came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when she told you what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I think? I didn't expect my sister to be alive, too. It was really a surprise. It was by sheer chance that I found my sister. If I would have left Poland at that time, I would never know that my sister is alive because I would never find her. It was a-- it was just a split of a moment that I decide to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your grandmothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, they died in the ghetto. So did my mother. My father was shot. And so did my sister-- my sister, Hana, died in the ghetto uprising. And that was it. And from Canada, I came to this country. My sister remained in Canada. She-- she married. She's happily married. She has four children. She has three grandchildren. And today, she is visiting Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your sister, Regina.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my sister, Regina. My sister Hana died during the ghetto uprising. So we-- from the whole family, we the only two left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was your family, your extended family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had several aunts and uncles, and 50, maybe 50 cousins, maybe more. Nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Between aunts and uncles and cousins, how many were in your [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 70, 80 people. Who remembers?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something you'd like to say about your sister and your parents at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, What can I say? I-- even as old as I am now, I still miss my parents. I still, many times, think how nice would be that-- thanks god I came to the best country in the world and I'm comfortable. And how nice would it be if I would have my parents now and I could give them things that they never had. But it wasn't to be. And I am very close with my sister. And we see each other quite often, and her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came from Austria to the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], to Canada. And from Canada, I came legally into this country. My husband had a brother here, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: --he send us-- I was in Canada [SIGHS] 3 and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. It I work hard. And I came to this country. I went to work, too. After I was in this country 10 days, I went to work and I worked all the time, till I retired 11 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was your occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a dressmaker. I also lost my husband here, my first husband, in 1959. He was only 44 years old. I lost him due to an accident at work. And in 1961, I remarried. I raised my-- I helped raise my husband's three boys. All of them are highly educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- after your husband died, how did you make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still worked. I worked to the day until I married Eddie Hudes. Then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was May 27, 1961.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you-- what-- what accident did your husband had at-- have at work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a carpenter. And he fell off from a friend's scaffold. And he broke his ankle, smashed his heel. And during surgery, they were trying-- they-- when he had the emergency, and they put it together. But it wasn't right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was suffering for 16 month. And he went to have it redone, and never came out of the hospital. The death certificate said that he died of acute branchopneumonia following orthopedic surgery. But I have a suspicion that it wasn't true. They probably gave him too much anesthetic. He passed away at 44.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all our children, things got comfortable. I married my husband. He had three small boys, 8 and 1/2, 10 and 1/2, and 12 and 1/2. And I made three bar mitzvahs. I helped raise them. One is a professor of biostatistic-- Mark, the oldest one, in Berkeley.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's your stepson?

speaker: interviewee
text: My stepson. My middle stepson is vice president from the promotion department at Kaiser Permanente. Erwin, my youngest stepson, he's a lawyer. He is a partner in a law firm in Irvine. My own children, my Jacob is-- he works about 22 years for United Airlines. He begin as a maintenance man and is now a supervisor. My son Aron is a journalistic photographer. He works for the Jewish Federation Council in Miami. He also work for the Dade County Jewish paper and for the Broward County Jewish paper. I have-- I have five grandchildren. My oldest is Rachelle. She finished college in Chico. She works for a telephone company. My next is Debbie, my Aron's daughter. She is-- she's a doctor. She's optometrist. Is married to a doctor which is a podiatrist and just had a little baby, which made me a great-grandmother. Then is my granddaughter, Darlene. She finish college and she works in a doctor's office. Then there's my granddaughter Liana. And she finished as doctor's assistant. And she works for a dentist in Miami. And my grandson, Sam, he was just-- he just graduated from UC Davis. And he-- he got his bachelor's degree in economics. And he's going to go for-- for master's degree. He wants to be a CPA. So all the children are well-to-do. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- you've been married for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I just-- I am married-- just celebrated my 35th birthday to Eddie. I was married 20 years to Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're changing tapes now.
